passages.explanation: Jesus warns his disciples what's coming: they'll be expelled from synagogues, and people who kill them will believe they're serving God. This will happen because the persecutors don't know the Father or Jesus. He's telling them now so they'll remember when it happens. He says it's actually better for them that he leaves — if he doesn't go, the Counsellor (Holy Spirit) won't come. When the Spirit comes, he will convict the world on three counts: sin, because they don't believe in Jesus; righteousness, because Jesus is going to the Father and won't be visibly present to vindicate himself; and judgment, because the ruler of this world has already been judged.

Jesus says he has more to tell them but they can't handle it yet. When the Spirit of truth comes, he will guide them into all truth. The Spirit won't speak on his own authority — he'll speak what he hears and will reveal what's coming. The Spirit will glorify Jesus by taking what belongs to Jesus and declaring it to the disciples. Everything the Father has belongs to Jesus, which is why the Spirit draws from Jesus to teach them.

Jesus says in a little while they won't see him, and then a little while later they will see him again. The disciples are confused and discuss among themselves what he means. Jesus knows they want to ask and addresses it directly: they will weep and grieve while the world rejoices. But their grief will turn to joy. He compares it to childbirth — a woman suffers during labour but forgets the pain once the child is born. Their sorrow now will become a joy that no one can take from them. He's preparing them for the crucifixion and the resurrection that follows.

Jesus says that after these things, they won't need to ask him questions anymore. Whatever they ask the Father in his name will be given to them. Until now they haven't asked anything in his name — he tells them to ask so their joy can be complete. He acknowledges he's been speaking in figurative language, but the time is coming when he'll speak plainly about the Father. He won't need to pray to the Father on their behalf — the Father himself loves them because they've loved Jesus and believed he came from God. He states his journey plainly: he came from the Father into the world, and now he's leaving the world and going back to the Father.

The disciples say he's finally speaking plainly and they believe he came from God. Jesus questions their confidence: do you really believe now? The time is coming — and has already arrived — when they'll scatter, each going their own way, and leave him alone. Yet he's not alone because the Father is with him. He closes with the purpose behind everything he's said: so they can have peace in him. In the world they will have trouble. But they should take heart — he has overcome the world.
